planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 1–2 inches deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade in zone 8–11; Black Ginger (Krachai Dam, Thai Ginseng) is a compact, deciduous ginger-family perennial from Thailand and Laos grown for its dark-purple-to-black, aromatic rhizomes — one of Thailand's most important medicinal plants used for energy, vitality, and adaptation, and now internationally recognized for compounds supporting exercise performance and male health.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions just 1–2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to dappled light; Kaempferia parviflora is compact and low-growing — dies completely back to dormant rhizomes in winter and re-emerges in spring; mulch in zone 8–9 to protect rhizomes from hard frost. Kaempferia parviflora (Black Ginger, Krachai Dam, or Thai Ginseng) is a compact, deciduous, rhizomatous perennial native to Laos and northern Thailand, traditionally cultivated at altitude in the Chiang Mai and Mae Hong Son highlands, and one of the most important medicinal plants in Thai and Lao traditional herbal medicine. The small, dark-purple to nearly black rhizomes (distinctive in color compared to all other Kaempferia species) contain a unique array of methoxyflavones — particularly 5,7-dimethoxyflavone and 3,5,7,3',4'-pentamethoxyflavone — that have been extensively researched for anti-fatigue, adaptogenic, vasodilatory, and exercise-performance properties. Black Ginger is now marketed internationally as a sports supplement and traditional herbal tonic. Small, pale-purple, orchid-like flowers are held just above the ground among attractive, broad, ground-level leaves from spring through summer. An excellent partial-shade novelty herb for zone 8–11 gardens; the compact size makes it ideal for container growing in cooler climates.
sources: Thai Traditional Medicine — Kaempferia parviflora (Krachai Dam)

planting.note: Plant bulbils or divide rhizomes in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade in zone 9–12; Yellow Dancing Ladies Ginger is a delicate, shade-loving ginger-family perennial native to Thailand, Myanmar, Laos, and Southeast Asian forests, producing graceful, drooping inflorescences of small bright-yellow flowers with reflexed yellow petals and yellow stamens dancing on arching stems in summer — an outstanding shade-garden ornamental.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade with consistent moisture; Globba schomburgkii is shade-adapted and suffers in direct sunlight — the best location is filtered light beneath trees or in deep shade of structures, with consistently moist soil and high humidity. Globba schomburgkii (Yellow Dancing Ladies Ginger) is one of dozens of graceful, shade-tolerant Globba species native to the humid forests of Southeast Asia (Thailand, Myanmar, Laos, Cambodia), where these delicate perennials are adapted to deep forest-floor understory light conditions. The inflorescences are distinctively graceful — arching, pendulous racemes of small, bright-yellow to golden-yellow, two-lipped flowers on dangling, pedicellate stalks that dance in the lightest air movement, explaining the poetic common name 'Dancing Ladies.' Many Globba species also produce curious bulbils (small, rounded, violet-purple or green buds) in the inflorescence that can be collected and grown into new plants. The foliage is also attractive — neat, alternately arranged, lanceolate leaves on upright, reedy stems. Plants die back to dormant rhizomes in winter, re-emerging reliably in spring. Among the most effective and unusual shade-garden ornamentals for zone 9–12 warm climates. Plant en masse under trees or shade structures for the most impactful display.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Globba schomburgkii
Marie Selby Botanical Gardens — Zingiberaceae Collection

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in moist, fertile, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade; Cherapu is a small to medium Garcinia fruit tree endemic to Peninsular Malaysia bearing small, thin-skinned, orange to mandarin-orange-colored fruits with sweet-sour, soft, orange-yellow aroid pulp — among the most popular small Garcinia fruits of Malaysian tropical gardens and markets.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Garcinia prainiana requires consistent moisture, high humidity, and protection from wind and cold — mulch well and water regularly throughout the growing season. Garcinia prainiana (Cherapu or Button Mangosteen) is an evergreen fruit tree endemic to the lowland and hill forests of Peninsular Malaysia and southern Thailand, member of the Garcinia family (Clusiaceae) and closely related to the famous Mangosteen (Garcinia mangostana). The fruits are distinctive: small (1.5–2 inch), round, with thin, orange to mandarin-colored skin (not the thick, purple rind of mangosteen) that peels easily to reveal 4–5 segments of soft, sweet-sour, orange-to-white, juicy, aromatic pulp with a complex tropical flavor — often described as a blend of sweet orange, lychee, and tangy passion fruit. The tree is compact and ornamental with glossy, dark-green, opposite leaves, and suits smaller tropical gardens better than the slower-growing mangosteen. Like all Garcinia species, G. prainiana is slow-growing and requires patience — typically 5–8 years from grafted stock to first fruit. A prized and rarely exported tropical fruit tree for serious tropical fruit collectors in zone 10–12.

subcategory: Southeast Asian coastal ornamental fruit tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in moist, fertile, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Sea Apple is a large, handsome, coastal rainforest tree native to the Malay Peninsula, Borneo, and surrounding region, widely planted in Singapore and Malaysia as a shade and ornamental street tree for its attractive form, large glossy leaves, and spectacular masses of white powder-puff flowers followed by round, red, edible apple-like fruits.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Syzygium grande is fast-growing and tolerates brief waterlogging and coastal conditions — water regularly in establishment, then water deeply and infrequently once the tree is established. Syzygium grande (Sea Apple or Jambu Bol) is a large, handsome, fast-growing evergreen tree native to the coastal and freshwater-edge rainforests of the Malay Peninsula, Borneo, Sumatra, and the Philippines — one of the most important and beautiful tropical street and shade trees of Singapore and Peninsular Malaysia, where it is widely planted in parks, streets, and public spaces as one of the city's signature urban trees. The tree is spectacular in flower: enormous, cloud-like masses of small, white, 'powder-puff' flowers (characteristic of the Syzygium genus) cover the tree canopy in brilliant white floral displays multiple times per year. The round, green-to-pink-red fruits — 1–2 inches across, resembling small apples — are crisp, watery, mildly sweet, and edible fresh in the same way as the related rose apple (Syzygium jambos), though less flavorful. The fruit is also attractive to birds and wildlife. Syzygium grande is among the fastest-growing and most heat- and humidity-tolerant of the large Syzygium species and is well-suited to the humid tropical climate of coastal south Florida and Hawaii.
sources: Forest Research Institute Malaysia — Syzygium grande
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Syzygium grande

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained, fertile to average soil in zone 8–11; Governor's Plum is a thorny, fast-growing shrub or small tree native to tropical Africa and Asia producing small, round, deep-red to dark-purple berries with a sweet-astringent-plum flavor widely eaten across its range — a tough, drought-tolerant fruiting shrub adaptable to poor soils and also valued as an impenetrable, thorny security hedge.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile to poor soil; Flacourtia indica is drought tolerant once established, adaptable to a wide range of soil types including lateritic and calcareous soils, and grows vigorously with minimal care — prune to shape after fruiting and to maintain desired height. Flacourtia indica (Governor's Plum, Ramontchi, or Madagascar Plum) is a thorny, fast-growing, many-branched shrub or small tree native to tropical and subtropical Africa and South Asia from Madagascar, the East African coast, and sub-Saharan Africa east through India, Sri Lanka, and Southeast Asia to the Philippines. The small (0.5–1 inch), round to slightly flattened fruits ripen from green through red to deep red-purple or nearly black, with tart-sweet, juicy, dark-red flesh eaten fresh, made into jams, jellies, syrups, and fermented into wine across its broad native range. The flesh is astringent when underripe but richly sweet-tart and plum-like when fully ripe. Governor's Plum is a tough, adaptable, undemanding fruiting plant tolerant of poor soils, drought, alkalinity, and partial neglect — an excellent productive hedgerow plant or edible security hedge plant for zone 8–11. The plants are dioecious (separate male and female trees required for fruit set). Naturalized and sometimes invasive in tropical regions outside its range.
sources: Morton, J. — Fruits of Warm Climates (1987)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 9b–12) in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Ambarella (Golden Apple, June Plum) is a fast-growing tropical fruit tree native to the Society Islands of the South Pacific, widely cultivated throughout tropical Asia, the Caribbean, and tropical Americas for its oval, golden-yellow fruits with crisp, tart, pineapple-mango-flavored flesh eaten green as a condiment or ripe as a sweet fruit.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Spondias cytherea is fast-growing and drought tolerant once established — water deeply and infrequently in subtropical conditions; excellent performance in South Florida's calcareous soils and in Gulf Coast sandy soils. Spondias cytherea (Ambarella, Golden Apple, or June Plum) is a large, fast-growing, deciduous tropical tree native to the Society Islands of French Polynesia, spread throughout tropical Polynesia by Polynesian voyagers and later widely planted across tropical Asia, the Caribbean, Central and South America, and tropical West Africa. The oval, 2–3 inch, golden-yellow fruits have crisp, fibrous, firm, juicy, tart-sweet, aromatic flesh with flavors of unripe mango, pineapple, and apple — remarkably refreshing as a fresh fruit when ripe (golden-yellow), and extensively used green (firm, crunchy, very tart) as a condiment, relish, chutney base, and snack with salt, fish sauce, and chili across South and Southeast Asia. In the Caribbean (Jamaica) it is 'June Plum,' in Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia 'Ambarella.' Trees are fast-growing — fruiting in 3–4 years from cuttings — and highly productive, dropping fruit that must be gathered regularly. One of the easiest tropical fruit trees to establish in zone 9b–12; an outstanding choice for South Florida, the Gulf Coast, and similar warm subtropical climates where it naturalizes readily.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Fruit Guide — Ambarella
Morton, J. — Fruits of Warm Climates (1987)
